experiments.title: How Interrupting the Browsing Experience with a Gift Selection Slide-up Impacts Online Donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a gift selection slide-up for website visitors on Enter the Bible will achieve an increase in donation conversions because interrupting passive browsing with a clear and immediate giving opportunity reduces friction and prompts action through attention capture and choice simplification.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2025 - 03/20/2026
Luther Seminary operates several high-traffic digital properties, with Enter the Bible receiving approximately 450,000 visitors within 30 days—which is more than its other platforms. Given this scale, the team identified Enter the Bible as the most viable environment to test new on-site fundraising tactics that could generate meaningful learning and results.
Historically, the site has relied on relatively passive donation opportunities, which do not actively interrupt or redirect user attention. This raises an important question: are visitors motivated to give when presented with a more prominent and immediate fundraising prompt, or does this type of interruption create friction that reduces engagement?
To explore this, the team is testing the impact of a more attention-grabbing on-site element: a gift selection slide-up. This element appears during the browsing experience and is designed to actively divert user attention toward making a donation.
The experiment includes two variations:
Control: The existing website experience with no additional fundraising interruption
Treatment: A site-wide slide-up featuring three predefined donation amounts and an additional “other” option
This test is being run across general website visitors to Enter the Bible, leveraging the platform’s high traffic volume to reach a large and diverse audience.
The goal of this experiment is to better understand donor motivation in a passive content environment. Specifically, whether introducing a more assertive, attention-directing prompt increases donation activity, or whether it disrupts the user experience in a way that suppresses giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrates that a more interruptive, attention-grabbing donation prompt can drive incremental giving on a high-traffic content site.

The gift selection slide-up (treatment) generated 8 donations totaling $347.80, with an average gift of $43.48, while the control generated no recorded donations. This suggests that proactively prompting users can convert a small portion of otherwise passive visitors into donors.

While VWO reported a +0.03% lift with 99.5% confidence, the results required additional validation. Because users were redirected from the Enter the Bible domain to a Luther Seminary donation page without cross-domain tracking enabled, conversions were not fully captured within the testing platform.

To account for this, a backup campaign tracking setup within the donation system was used to verify results. Although originally implemented to keep the test clean, this approach ultimately ensured accurate attribution and confirmed that all donations came from the treatment experience.

From a behavioral perspective, the results suggest that capturing attention and simplifying the giving decision through preset gift options can prompt action, even when initial donor intent is low. However, the low overall conversion rate indicates that attention alone is not enough—stronger motivational framing will likely be required to drive meaningful scale.

Next Steps
Keep the Donation Experience Within a Single Domain: Future tests will prioritize directing users to donation pages within the Enter the Bible environment. Keeping the full journey within a single, first-party domain will improve tracking accuracy, reduce attribution gaps, and enable more confident decision-making.
 	Test Alternative On-Site Donation Prompts: Build on this learning by testing additional formats, such as a sticky donation slide-out, to understand how different levels of persistence and visibility impact conversion.
 	Strengthen Motivational Framing: Pair attention-grabbing elements with messaging that emphasizes urgency, impact, or donor identity to increase both conversion rate and overall revenue.

experiments.title: How a testimonial email from a donor performed against a faux-forward email during a fundraising appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donor testimonial email from a real donor for people receiving a campaign appeal email will achieve an increase in email clicks to the donation page because personal stories can increase social proof and encourage readers to join them by becoming a donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
During the critical calendar year-end fundraising period, Luther Seminary wanted to test the effectiveness of their Giving Tuesday PM email communication strategy. The traditional approach has used a faux-forward email, where the PM message includes a “forwarded” copy of the AM email, with a brief added note from the person forwarding it.
For this experiment, we tested the faux-forward strategy against a Treatment email that replaced the forwarded content with a testimonial from a real donor, Greg, sharing his personal reasons for supporting Luther Seminary. This test aimed to determine whether focusing on a donor testimonial would lead to higher engagement and more conversions compared to the repetitive messaging of the faux-forward email.
Donor stories would create stronger emotional connections and trust, motivating readers to act and contribute to Luther Seminary’s mission. A personal endorsement from a peer would resonate more deeply with recipients, leading to increased clicks to the donation page. At the same time, the faux-forward approach might contribute to disengagement or message fatigue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we found that the Treatment email, featuring a testimonial from a donor (Greg), led to a -39% decrease in clicks to the donation page, with a 97.9% statistical level of confidence. This result was unexpected, as we hypothesized that the personal donor story would increase trust and emotional connection, ultimately driving more clicks to the donation page.

One major factor contributing to this decrease could be the -21.8% decrease in email opens for the Treatment email, with a 100% statistical level of confidence. This drop in opens suggests that the unfamiliar sender, Greg Myhr, may have impacted the email's ability to capture the recipient’s attention. People are more likely to open emails from familiar senders, a concept known as sender recognition or trust transfer. In this case, emails from Luther Seminary staff may have felt more familiar and trustworthy, prompting more recipients to open the email.

Psychologically, cognitive fluency plays a role here: people are more likely to engage with content that feels easy, familiar, and comfortable. When the email is from a known and trusted source (like Luther Seminary staff), recipients may feel more at ease engaging with the content. When the email comes from an unfamiliar source (like a donor), it may subconsciously signal uncertainty or a potential risk, causing people to hesitate or overlook the email altogether.

Additionally, while social proof can be a powerful motivator, it needs to be framed in a way that maximizes its impact. Although Greg’s testimonial aimed to leverage the principle of social proof by showing how a fellow donor supports Luther Seminary, the email’s unfamiliar sender might have diminished its credibility. In-group bias suggests that people tend to trust and engage more with others who belong to their perceived social group or network. Because Greg was an outsider to the recipients (compared to the staff sender), the emotional pull of his testimonial may have been weakened.

Another contributing factor could be cognitive overload. The Treatment email included a personal story from Greg, which while emotionally powerful, might have added a layer of complexity or unfamiliarity that decreased its effectiveness compared to the more straightforward faux-forward email (the Control). The faux-forward email, which came from a familiar source, was simpler and less cognitively demanding for the reader, making it easier to process and act upon.

This suggests that while donor testimonials are effective in fostering connection and trust, the familiarity of the sender is crucial in determining how much attention and engagement the email receives. If the recipient doesn't recognize or trust the sender immediately, they may be less likely to open the email and, as a result, less likely to engage with the content, including the donation link.

experiments.title: How testing whether an urgency-driven subject line or a specific biblical reference leads to higher opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a specific and intriguing biblical reference (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) instead of a more generic faith-based phrase (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because specificity and curiosity drive engagement, making the subject line feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/08/2025
Luther Seminary creates ebooks and digital resources to help their audience deepen their faith and biblical knowledge. Their latest ebook explores the book of Exodus, and they aimed to maximize engagement by optimizing the subject line of their promotional email to their housefile audience.
To determine what type of subject line would drive more opens and clicks, we conducted a series of A/B tests. In a prior test, we compared exclusivity-based language to a curiosity-driven subject line, finding that a curiosity-driving subject line increased opens by 9.4% with 100% statistical confidence.
For this next test, we wanted to know whether an urgency-based subject line (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) or a subject line that was more specific and intriguing (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) would drive higher engagement. We hypothesized that the more specific and curiosity-driven subject line would increase opens by making the content feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test produced unexpected but valuable insights about the relationship between subject line messaging and user engagement.

While the Control subject line ("Don't miss this opportunity to deepen your faith") generated more opens, the Treatment subject line ("Discover the richness of Exodus") resulted in fewer opens (a 3.8% decrease) but led to a 24.5% increase in email clicks, with a 98.4% statistical level of confidence.

This suggests that while urgency-driven language in the Control created a stronger initial impulse to open the email, the specificity of the Treatment subject line attracted a more engaged audience—those who opened were more likely to click through to the content.

Psychologically, this aligns with two key principles:
FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) and urgency – The Control subject line may have driven more opens by making the email feel time-sensitive and broadly relevant.
 	Relevance and commitment – The Treatment subject line set clearer expectations about the content, likely attracting recipients who were already interested in the topic, leading to higher engagement post-open.

experiments.title: How comparing a curiosity-driven subject line to an exclusivity-based subject line to determine impact on opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a vague, curiosity-driven subject line (“Here’s your first look”) instead of an exclusivity-based subject line (“Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because the curiosity gap encourages recipients to open the email to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/17/2025
Luther Seminary develops useful ebooks and other digital resources that help their audience deepen their faith and knowledge of the Bible. They recently created a new ebook about the book of Exodus and wanted to send it to their housefile email audience.
We wanted to know what kind of subject line would drive more opens and clicks for this content offer email, so we decided to run an A/B test.
The Control subject line used exclusivity messaging: “Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide.” The Treatment subject line was shorter and designed to spark curiosity: “Here’s your first look.”
We hypothesized that the curiosity-driven subject line would generate more opens by enticing recipients to learn more.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment confirmed that a curiosity-driven subject line can significantly increase email opens. The Treatment subject line ("Here's your first look") outperformed the Control ("Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide") by increasing opens 9.4%, with a 100% statistical level of confidence.

The success of the curiosity-based subject line aligns with the Curiosity Gap Theory, which suggests that people feel compelled to seek out missing information when presented with an incomplete or intriguing statement. Unlike the Control, which relied on exclusivity messaging, the Treatment sparked interest without revealing too much—prompting recipients to open the email to learn more.

However, while opens increased, click rates remained the same (~2.2% for both versions). This suggests that while curiosity successfully drove more people to open the email, it did not necessarily increase engagement with the content inside. A possible explanation is that the Treatment subject line created intrigue but did not set clear expectations about the offer, leading some recipients to open the email without a strong intention to engage further.

To build on this learning, future tests should explore:
Refining curiosity-driven subject lines – Testing variations that maintain intrigue while hinting more explicitly at the content's value.
 	Combining curiosity with faith-based language – Testing curiosity-driven phrasing with biblical references to see if it enhances both opens and clicks. Update: We ran a follow-up experiment to test curiosity-based language against a subject line featuring a specific biblical reference to see which approach is more effective at engaging faith-driven audiences.
 	Aligning email content with subject line expectations – Ensuring that the email itself delivers on the curiosity generated in the inbox, potentially increasing clicks.

experiments.title: How using direct, biblically-aligned language in a Facebook ebook ad impacted sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using direct, biblically-aligned language in the opening of our Facebook ads will increase email sign-ups for the ebook because it makes the offer feel more interesting, accessible, and relevant to potential readers seeking spiritual growth.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 10/28/2024
Luther Seminary was running Facebook ads to promote their free ebook about the book of Numbers. We wanted to test whether a shorter, clearer opening paragraph in the ad would lead to more engagement and email signups. Our theory was that the Control ad might scare users away because it used phrases like “daunting hike” and “endless desert”.
For the treatment language, we focused on getting the reader’s attention by referencing the content of the book of Numbers and using more biblically-aligned language to describe the journey of the people of Israel (“guided through the wilderness” vs the control that said “daunting hike…endless desert”).
Our hypothesis was that using direct, biblically-aligned language in our Facebook ads would increase email sign-ups for the ebook because:
It creates a vivid, authentic connection to scripture, 
Evokes a sense of divine guidance by mentioning God’s guidance, and 
Reduces anxiety by making the offer feel more accessible and relevant to potential readers seeking spiritual growth.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using direct, biblically-aligned language in the opening of our Facebook ads can increase email sign-ups for an biblical study guide ebook. The treatment ad, which focused on referencing the content of the book of Numbers and using clearer, more biblically-aligned language, led to a valid 22.1% in email sign-ups with a 99.2% level of confidence (LOC).
We also saw that the treatment ads drove a 225.5% increase in instant donations with a 97.4% level of confidence!
When thinking about the donor psychology behind these results, these are some possible reasons for why the treatment ads performed better:
Authentic Biblical Imagery: The use of traditional biblical language like "wilderness" and the reference to "walking alongside the people of Israel" resonated strongly with the target audience who are familiar with biblical narratives, appealing to those seeking a genuine, faith-based study experience.Divine Guidance and Protection: Mentioning "God guided them through the wilderness" taps into the core desire for divine guidance and protection, which adds to the spiritual significance and relevance.Accessibility and Curiosity: The simpler, more straightforward language reduces anxiety by making the offer feel more accessible. The phrase "walking alongside" suggests a less intimidating, observational role compared to the language in the control ad.
In future experimentation, Luther Seminary should continue to use direct, biblically-aligned language in their marketing materials to create a vivid, authentic connection to scripture, evoke a sense of divine guidance, and make the offer feel more accessible and relevant to potential readers seeking spiritual growth. Additionally, since instant donations also saw a significant increase with the treatment language, it may be worthwhile to explore how this language can be incorporated into donation campaigns as well.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page during a high-urgency direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-up with a gift array for users on Luther’s Working Preacher site will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2024 - 10/17/2024
Luther Seminary was running a high-urgency campaign to support their Working Preacher site, and one of the web elements on the site was a standard direct appeal sticky bar. We wanted to test whether using a slide-up element instead of a sticky bar would drive more user engagement through clicks to the donation page.
The sticky bar and slide-up both used the same language, but the slide-up presented users with 3 gift options. Clicking a gift option would direct users to the donation page and the form would be pre-filled with their selected gift amount.
Our hypothesis was that the slide-up would drive an increase in clicks to the donation page because it lets users begin the process of giving before they even land on the donation page. We also hoped that this would result in a downstream lift in donations from the slide-up.
We split the website traffic 50/50 between the two variants, and the test ran for ~3 days before we ran out of testing credits and had to end it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a slide-up with a gift array on Luther Seminary's Working Preacher site led to a significant increase in clicks to the donation page compared to a standard sticky bar. The treatment resulted in a 43.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence (99.5%)!
We also found that the slide-up led to a 33.4% increase in donor conversion, but this was only with a 68.3% level of confidence.
From this test we learned that it is important to continue exploring different ways to engage Working Preacher users and streamline the donation process for them. It is clear that providing users with the option to select a gift amount before landing on the donation page can be effective in driving more clicks and potentially increasing donations. For future testing, we want to experiment with the language in the slide-up, as well as the psychology of price anchoring with our gift array amounts.

experiments.title: How a personalized subject line impacted engagement on a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the exclusivity and appeal for the housefile audience content offer recipients will achieve an increase in engagement because there is more relevance to the individual recipient communicated in the subject line.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2024 - 07/08/2024
When sending an email to the housefile with a content offer, we wondered if increasing the exclusivity and appeal of the email’s subject line would impact engagement from the audience. We used “One popular method for learning and sharing the gospel” as the control subject line and “Exclusive opportunity for you, Patty” dynamically inserting the recipient’s first name as the treatment and measured the click rate on both versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the exclusivity and appeal of the email's subject line by personalizing it with the recipient's first name can significantly impact engagement from the audience. The treatment version, which included the recipient's first name, outperformed the control version in terms of click rates by 40.3% with a 100% level of confidence, indicating that the treatment was more effective in driving engagement.

In future experimentation, testing different levels of personalization or combining personalization with other compelling elements in the subject line could help optimize engagement.

When sending a content offer to the housefile, we wondered if we could increase response and engagement of the message by increasing the perceived value of the offer through adding exclusivity and personalization in the subject line vs. a subject line that merely invoked curiosity around the contents and its potential usefulness or benefit to the visitor.

By creating a sense of exclusivity and personalization in the subject line, the perceived value of the content offer is increased, prompting more recipients to engage with the email. In future experimentation, it is important to continue utilizing personalized and exclusive language in subject lines to increase engagement with email content and potentially improve overall campaign performance. This could involve tailoring subject lines based on recipient behavior or preferences, creating a sense of VIP access, or offering unique opportunities specifically for housefile recipients. By leveraging the power of personalization and exclusivity, nonprofits like Luther Seminary can effectively communicate with their audience and drive higher levels of engagement with their messaging.

experiments.title: How a new email sender impacts click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a new sender will produce a higher click-through rate, because it will increase curiosity among recipients, due to the email being sent from a new name.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2024 - 01/20/2024
Luther Seminary wanted to start sending housefile emails using a new employee. Instead of just switching to the new employee immediately, we tested how the housefile reacted to this new sender. We tested Luther Seminary’s normal housefile email sender vs. the new sender using a cultivation housefile email. For this test, we wanted to see how the new sender impacted the click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a new sender for housefile emails can significantly impact engagement. In this case, the treatment group saw a 21.6% increase in click-through rate and a 10.6% increase in opens compared to the control group.
This suggests that the new sender created curiosity among recipients, encouraging them to engage with the email. The results are statistically significant with a high level of confidence, indicating that this effect is likely to be real and not due to chance.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of using new senders on engagement metrics. This could involve using different employees or personas as email senders to see if the effect is consistent across different individuals or contexts.
Furthermore, it would be valuable to explore the long-term impact of using new senders. Do recipients continue to engage with emails from new senders over time, or does the effect diminish? Understanding the long-term effects can help refine the strategy for using new senders in housefile emails.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of considering the sender's identity in email marketing. It suggests that switching up the sender can generate curiosity and increase recipient engagement, ultimately leading to higher click-through and open rates.

experiments.title: How a specific donation ask in an email for non-donors impacted the email click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking non-donors for a gift of $10 or more instead of a gift of any amount will increase the click rate because of the added clarity in the copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2023 - 11/28/2023
Luther Seminary participated in Giving Tuesday and wanted to try to increase traffic to the donation page and hopefully also increase overall donations.
In order to achieve this, we wanted to see how adding a specified ask amount for nondonors would impact the click rate overall.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specified ask amount for nondonors significantly increased the click rate overall. The treatment version, which asked for a gift of $10 or more, resulted in a 57.2% increase in cilcks with a high level of confidence.
This suggests that the added clarity in the copy, specifying the amount to be donated, is effective in motivating people to click and donate. It gives potential donors a concrete expectation and may make them more likely to take action.
However, for the overall donor conversion rate, there was a 4.4% increase in donation conversions at a 14.1% level of confidence. This means that while we can say this factor did lead to an increase in clicks, we can't with confidence say that this will always lead to an increase in donations.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing and refining the specified ask amount. Different donation amounts could be tested to see which has the greatest impact on click rate and donations. Additionally, exploring other ways to increase clarity and motivation in the copy, such as highlighting the impact of the donation, could also be tested.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of clear and specific messaging in fundraising campaigns. Providing a specific ask amount can help potential donors understand what is being asked of them and may increase their likelihood of taking action.

experiments.title: How a timely and relevant subject line impacted clicks on a cultivation email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the relevancy of a subject line for cultivation email recipients will achieve an increase in email engagement because the timeliness of the message is increased.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2023 - 11/20/2023
When sending a cultivation email to the housefile, we wanted to see if we could increase clicks by calling attention in the subject line that the content of the message was seasonal and relevant. The control subject line drove curiosity “embracing the unpredictable” but we wondered if more people would engage with the email if we let them know it couldn’t wait to be opened or they would lose out on the timeliness of the article inside.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the relevancy of the subject line for cultivation email recipients resulted in a significant increase in email engagement, specifically the Clicks metric. The treatment subject line, which highlighted the timeliness of the article inside, outperformed the control subject line in terms of driving clicks and led to a 76% increase in clickthrough rate with 100% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it would be useful to explore subject lines that strike a balance between timeliness and curiosity. For example, a subject line that hints at seasonal and relevant content while also creating a sense of curiosity or intrigue might be effective in driving both opens and clicks.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of continuously testing and optimizing subject lines to maximize email engagement. By using data-driven insights, we can better understand what drives recipient behavior and tailor our messaging accordingly.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition to focus on “more content like this” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the value proposition on “more content like this” for online course enrollees will achieve an increase in conversion rate  because  specificity helps decrease cognitive friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2023 - 03/26/2023
Luther Seminary has run this experiment across multiple of their content offers running in paid acquisition through Facebook including study guides and an online course for Revelation. They recently launched a new online course about the Book of Psalms, so we wanted to test this concept on the instant donation page to see if it would produce the same lift in conversion as it did for Revelation (834.6% lift with a 99.1% LoC).
The control donation page focused on reasons to give that were given were very high-level, wide-ranging, and broad. We hypothesized that if they focused the value proposition on just one element—particularly, the online courses that the user had just signed up for, they could improve donor conversion rate.
So, we drafted a new donation page with this “micro-value-proposition” and launched a test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for more than three weeks, the single-value-proposition treatment produced a lift in conversion rate by 105.2% with a 98.2% level of confidence.
This experiment demonstrated that Luther Seminary might significantly increase its donation rate by focusing the value proposition on a single element — in this case, the online course it promotes. By providing a "micro-value-proposition," which is more targeted and relevant, it helps decrease cognitive friction and increase conversions.
Furthermore, the experiment showed that the treatment was particularly effective for email visitors, desktop visitors, and returning visitors. Email visitors saw a 1564% increase in donations, desktop visitors had a 1350% increase in donations, and returning visitors had a massive 4817% increase in donations.
In future experimentation, organizations should focus on implementing a more targeted and relevant value proposition to improve conversion rates.

experiments.title: How framing your donation ask on an instant donation page around providing funding for other free resources affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a donation to fund the production of the next free biblical resource for study guide downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they saw the value in downloading the first biblical resource on the previous landing page therefore they’re more likely to want to see other biblical resources be produced in the future .

Timeframe: 01/25/2023 - 02/19/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a free study guide on the book of Revelation through paid spend in Facebook.
In order to try and increase the donor conversion rate on the instant donation page, they wanted to test asking for a donation to fund other free biblical resources to be produced in the future. The treatment page also presented a “premium” in a way that if they gave a donation of $10 or more, they would be the first to receive access to the next biblical resource created.

The treatment page saw a 109.7% increase in donor conversion rate with a 97.3% level of confidence which makes us believe that these study guide downloaders are more likely to make a donation on the instant donation page when presented with the offer to receive another free biblical resource in the future, which they are helping fund the production of.

The treatment page also saw a 153.7% lift in revenue with a 95.9% level of confidence. We believe these donors are likely to give larger gifts when presented with what their donation will actually fund aka their next free resource.

These results suggest that offering future "premiums" is an effective way of encouraging donors.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array options for an acquisition offer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array options for people who downloaded a study guide will achieve a higher donor conversion rate on the instant donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2022 - 11/19/2022
Luther Seminary has been running an acquisition offer on Facebook for a free study guide for the book of Revelation. We previously tested the concept of lowering the gift array on the instant donation page for acquisition offers, so we wanted to further test this concept for this offer as well.
As you can see in the images below, the gift array options ranged wide and high for an offer with perceived low value that was ultimately being presented to new names and potentially new donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The lower gift array resulted in an 88.5% increase in donor conversion rate with a 99.9% LoC. We believe this is because the value of the content offer received was not equal to the value of donation options given on the original donation page. The options presented in the treatment were far more tangible to someone who is brand new coming onto the Luther Seminary file as a majority of our samples were new site visitors.

Another notable takeaway is how the lower gift array affected overall revenue. We saw a 52% increase in revenue with a 96% level of confidence on the treatment page because the lift in conversion rate was so high. The lower gift array did see a lower average gift of about $6, but overall, that does not affect our revenue from this offer.

We believe this is because of how people view the level of complexity and perceived value each offer (and specifically the book of the Bible) provides to the consumer.

Furthermore, we'd like to continue to test this concept on new acquisition offers to see how best that audience responds.

experiments.title: How removing thank you from the header on the instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing thank you from the header on the donation page for course enrollees will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 11/11/2022
Luther Seminary is offering an online course to study the Book of Revelation. We had previously seen other organizations test the concept of removing “thank you” from the header of the donation page as that typically ends a user’s thought process when signing up for something or completing a purchase. Usually, when you see the thank you confirmation page, you think to yourself – Okay, I’m done now, so I’m leaving the page.
We predicted for this online course that users are leaving the donation page after seeing “thank you” at the very top, so we want to test changing that language so something that lets the user know that they are not done yet.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 132% increase in donor conversion rate with a 96% level of confidence. We believe by saying "But before you go, will you consider something?" that the user's thought process is no longer interrupted therefore they are more likely to stay on the page to read the value proposition rather than bouncing as soon as they see "thank you."
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 140.2% increase with a 97% level of confidence.
We believe revenue experienced such a high lift on the treatment page because we were able to keep double the amount of people on the page. After staying and reading the copy on why they should give, they were more likely to make a donation.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array options for an acquisition offer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array options for potential donors who just signed up for an online course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate on the instant donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 10/20/2022
Luther Seminary has been running an acquisition offer on Facebook for a free online course for the book of Revelation. The other acquisition offers they are offering are free study guides for the Book of Proverbs and Book of Revelation, so this online course was something different and new to give to their audience.
The performance of this online course shortly after launching it was very good with the ICR averaging at 4.7% and the GCPD averaging at $110.
We’ve been testing the lower gift array on both study guide funnels since the control gift array seems to be a high ask for an offer with a lower value. We decided to test this same concept on this online course since, historically, online courses are perceived to provide more value to potential donors.
We wanted to see if this audience would react any differently to seeing a lower gift array when offered a higher value free resource.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The lower gift array did not have an effect on donor conversion rate as we saw a 13.1% decrease in conversion rate with only a 43.3% LoC. Although, the lower gift array did have a large impact on average gift resulting in a 45.4% decrease in revenue with a 97.6% LoC.

We believe this happened because the donors on the control page saw the value of this online course therefore the lowest ask of $50 did not sway them from giving a gift. But the donors who saw the treatment page were more likely to give a smaller gift of $25.

We now can assume that donors are more likely to give higher gifts when presented the option to do so when the offer they received is of high value.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
New visitors had a 34.7% decrease in revenue with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 50.8% decrease in revenue with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 72.2% decrease in revenue with a 97% level of confidence.

rows.name: Campaign Focused Donation Page - Countdown Clock

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher contacts will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2022 - 10/27/2022
Luther Seminary was running its bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. They were sending traffic to this page via mainly campaign emails being sent to the Working Preacher contacts and donors. There was also a significant amount of direct traffic coming to this page from the Working Preacher website.
We wanted to test adding a countdown clock to the deadline of the campaign on October 31st to see if this would increase urgency and make more people likely to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The countdown clock resulted in a 20.2% decrease in donor conversion rate with a near-valid level of confidence of 94.9%. Also, revenue experienced a 40.8% decrease with a 99% level of confidence.

We believe this audience did not respond well to the countdown clock because we launched this experiment 14 days prior to the deadline therefore it did not give the audience enough urgency to give at that time. It actually deterred people from giving probably due to the fact that they still a one week to two weeks left to donate.

We would like to test this concept again for this audience but only show the countdown clock on the final days of the campaign to see if the urgency actually increases giving then.

experiments.title: How personalizing and removing heavy design affects high urgency campaign emails for donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing an email for alumni and potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 09/22/2022
Luther Seminary ran their annual 24-hour Give Day high urgency campaign from 9/21 – 9/22. The emails sent in this campaign were heavily designed and had a marketing feel rather than a personalized, conversation feel.
We wanted to test removing the design from the emails and changing the language to be more conversational from an actual person at the seminary.
We made these changes across the 9 emails for the alumni and all other segments since those were the only two segments large enough to test within.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment saw a 23.1% decrease in conversion rate with a 95.4% level of confidence across the 9 emails sent on Give Day. We concluded that Luther Seminary's audience is accustomed to the highly designed, marketing emails therefore the personalized emails did not increase the likelihood of someone giving a donation.
We believe that the timeline and words of inspiration at the top of the control email actually provided a sense of encouragement and urgency to give that day. Whereas the treatment emails were more focused on making a connection with the potential donor through conversation-like language.
Another notable takeaway from this experiment is that the click-through rates on the emails saw 19.7% decrease with a 98.2% level of confidence from the treatment emails.
We would like to further test this concept in another high-urgency campaign that spans a longer period of time. We believe this will provide more accurate information than a campaign that has 9 emails within 24 hours.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a digital acquisition donation page for prospective donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2022 - 08/25/2022
Luther Seminary is offering Enter the Bible: Revelation Study Guide as an acquisition offer through paid Facebook ads and to their housefile through email. We wanted to increase ICR on the instant donation page, so we decided to retest the humanized sticky bar on this page.
We hypothesized that this would increase donor conversion rate by humanizing the donation page with a message (and picture) from Katie Langston who is well known in the Enter the Bible community asking the user for a donation to be able to continue to provide resources like this one in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a humanized sticky bar actually led to a decrease in conversion rates of 56.4%.
This informs us that this humanized message actually has a negative effect on donor conversion rate and decreases the motivation to give a gift. We believe this is due to the lack of motivation to give throughout the full funnel therefore the presence of the sticky bar does not "convince" people to make a donation.
Further testing on messaging and subjects will be done in the future to see if we can get a lift. This experiment should be run again on an instant donation page that receives a high volume of traffic to the page.

Revenue experienced a 53.7% decrease with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects instant conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition language on the instant donation page for prospective donors will achieve clarity and cause an increase in ICR.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2022 - 08/28/2022
Luther Seminary has been running paid Facebook ads and email marketing for their content offer Enter the Bible: Proverbs Study Guide. While the email sign-up rate was healthy around 48%, we wanted to try and increase the instant conversion rate on the donation page.
We hypothesized that the copy on the control page did not provide clarity on the impact a donation has for Luther Seminary and what the donor’s gift will do for the organization.
We believed that adding more value proposition-focused language to the page would provide clarity to the prospective donor for how their gift will be used in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the experiment were encouraging when it comes to an increase in conversion rate for email traffic: 67.7 increase in conversion rate for email traffic with a level of confidence of 96.9%. When it came to the all-traffic segment, we saw a 59.1 increase in conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96.9%.
When it came to paid social traffic, the change of the language did not provide a valid or directional increase in conversion rate.
We believe the value prop-centric language had the most effect on users who know Luther Seminary well and are very aware of its mission and goals. When it came to new visitors (i.e. traffic from paid social), the language was not motivating enough to convince more people to donate to the Seminary.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by changing the confirmation message at the top of the page to be more clarifying following the previous step in the funnel. We will still want to verify these results by running this experiment on another instant donation page while isolating the variables being tested.

experiments.title: How using direct language in pop-up copy for a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that directly asking if Working Preacher is a helpful resource for people viewing the Working Preacher website will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2022 - 05/26/2022
Luther Seminary runs a high urgency campaign for Working Preacher every year during the fall and spring. Luther Seminary ran an A/B pop-up test during the fall campaign between copy about helping pastors receive the resources they need and copy about supporting the Working Preacher campaign. The copy around supporting the Working Preacher campaign and website saw a 7.9% lift with a 41.9% LoC.
For the spring campaign, they wanted to use copy around supporting the campaign and website, but they tested more detailed copy around what Working Preacher provides for users against shorter copy that more directly asked the user if Working Preacher helped them as an individual.
They crafted an alternate popup and split the traffic to see if it produced a conversion increase.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we observed was a 49.5% increase in donor conversion rate on the treatment with a 97.9% level of confidence. The results indicated that the Working Preacher audience responds more to a direct and specific ask about whether or not Working Preacher was valuable to them as an individual. Being direct on what results from their gift by helping them specifically grow closer to God and lead others vs being abstract by saying Working Preacher helps people and pastors all over the world will inspire more gifts leading to a higher donor conversion rate.
Another takeaway from this experiment is that mobile visitors had a 229.8% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence. We believe this to be caused by the treatment having shorter pop-up copy therefore the user was more inclined to click on the "Make your donation" button because the shorter copy caused less friction.

Revenue experienced a 74.8% increase with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How highlighting the last date given impacts donor retention rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the last date given for existing donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2022
Luther Seminary was running their Spring Working Preacher campaign. They knew that they had many people on their file who had given before, but had not given recently. One hypothesis they had was that donors think they have given more recently than they have.
Their original draft of the email had a callout about the impact the donor’s gift would have, and noted that their “participation at any level” would help continue to provide Working Preacher.
They tested the callout against an ask that noted the last time the donor had given, the amount they had given, and asked if they would make a gift of the same amount.  They split the file of previous donors and tested the two against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which called out the last date a donor made their gift, increased conversion by 71.8%. This echoes previous research that showed that, while lifecycle messaging is important, it can also be powerful to remind donors when they last gave.
There's a fine line here that must be ridden—you don't want to guilt people into giving, as they won't continue to give. But we must confront donors with the reality that time has passed since their last gift.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning a landing page with clarifying copy and removing navigation effects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the navigation and providing clarity in the page copy for users signing up for this online course will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 08/04/2022
Luther Seminary had been running their acquisition pages with navigation in the header and footer of the page. They wanted to test what would happen if we removed the navigation from the page to see if this would increase email acquisition.
We tested this concept on their Cosmic Adventure online course landing page. On top of this page having the navigation bars, the offer was not performing well for a consistent amount of time, so they also wanted to test changing the headline and body copy of the page to see if providing clarity for the user as to what they would receive in this course would help increase email acquisition.
The original page language was vague throughout the headline, sub-headline, and body copy. Within the new copy, we wanted to be very specific in the headline and subheadline by stating exactly what this course would do for them – give them answers to questions between science and faith through an informative course. Within the body copy, we wanted to focus on curiosity and clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment (treatment page 2) produced a valid increase in the email sign-up rate of 44.7% with a 96.6 level of confidence. In looking at all three variants, we believe that removing the navigation in the header and footer provided the biggest impact on our sign-up rate. On top of that, changing the page copy to be more clarifying for the user as to what this course will give them ultimately provided us with the valid increase needed.
It is recommended that this be tested on other acquisition pages separately, so we can see the true impact of removing navigation vs adding clarifying copy.

experiments.title: How Adding Value Proposition Language near the ‘Give’ Button on a Homepage Affects Conversion Rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language near the give button on a homepage for web traffic will achieve higher conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2021 - 12/29/2021
Although Luther Seminary has a large, bright donation callout on their homepage, we observed that it was not driving the expected level of donations.
Since there were other bright design elements competing for attention around the donation callout, and because Luther Seminary’s homepage is somewhat ‘busy’, with many navigation choices for visitors, we hypothesized that perhaps visitors weren’t being given enough of a reason to choose to donate, with so many other options from which to choose.
Previous experiments have shown that clearly communicating a value proposition motivates donors to give. In light of these past learnings, we decided to test how adding value proposition language near the ‘GIVE’ button on Luther Seminary’s homepage might improve conversion rates without necessitating a redesign of their homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running our experiment for 90 days, we observed a 21% decrease in conversion rate with an 82.5% level of confidence.
While this result was surprising, it does provide context and points the direction for future optimization.
Most interestingly, while returning visitors showed a 65% decrease in conversion rates, the new treatment did result in a 127% increase in donations from new traffic (including an impressive 295% lift in paid traffic donations).
This provides some interesting insights:
first, we can hypothesize that for first time visitors, especially those being acquired via paid traffic, the value proposition was helpful in inspiring them to give
second, there could be a variety of factors leading to the decrease in donations from returning traffic, one of which may be the sheer number of things competing for a visitor's attention on the homepage. A quick heuristic analysis of the homepage indicates that it is quite busy and may cause friction for returning visitors. However, an analysis of website data (such as page path, exit, and bounce rate reports), combined with scroll tracking / heat mapping, should be conducted to see what people are doing when they are landing on the homepage and where they are going when they click through
It is doubtful that the inclusion of helpful value prop language was the key motivating factor in reducing donations from returning visitors, but additional analysis would be required to determine this with a high level of certainty.

experiments.title: How removing video and simplifying the donation form affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the video and reordering and simplifying the donation process for website visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2021 - 11/25/2021
Luther Seminary was running their biannual Working Preacher donation campaign. The campaign had traditionally used the main WP donation page, with a special video and their standard donation page.
But based on previous research, the team wanted to test the impact of making two significant changes. First, they wanted to test the impact of removing the video, as video had shown to reduce conversion for other campaigns.
Secondly, they wanted to see how simplifying the donation process and reordering the steps would affect conversion. As it stood, the donation form had a thermometer, a gift array, and then asked for a comment. It made a recurring ask after the gift amount was chosen, which could lead to cognitive friction between the previously selected amount and the monthly ask. If someone selected a $100 amount, would they want to give that monthly? They wanted to test asking for the frequency of gift first, then following up with the amount. They also made the gift array decrease when monthly ask was selected to align with traditional monthly ask amounts. Finally, they removed the comments box, which presented unnecessary friction.
To understand the unique impact of these changes, they ran an A/B/C test where variant B only removed the video, and variant C removed the video and added the “tabbed” simplified form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment C, with the removed video and the reordered, simplified donation form produced a 52% increase in conversion rate, with 97.3% significance. Digging deeper into the data, they found two interesting facts that showed how this was derived.
First, email traffic (which has a higher motivation due to reading a promotional message about the campaign) was largely unchanged. But direct traffic, the source of the most donations, was where the 52% lift was found.
Secondly, these changes did not affect desktop traffic at all, looking at all channels. But mobile traffic skyrocketed, with a 177.6% increase with a 99% level of confidence.
So in short, large gains in direct traffic (which has lower motivations) and mobile traffic (which has device-specific restrictions and where a video takes over the entire screen) were the sources of lift for this winning treatment.
