experiments.title: Does Displaying Multiple Ministry Brands Strengthen Donor Confidence?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that displaying the logos of Luther Seminary’s affiliated ministry brands below the donation button for Facebook-driven content audiences will achieve a higher conversion rate and increased revenue, as visible scale and brand familiarity strengthen trust and signal broader mission impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2026
Luther Seminary operates multiple well-known ministry brands, including Enter the Bible, Working Preacher, and Faith+Lead. Each brand serves a distinct audience while advancing Luther’s broader mission of providing free, high-quality biblical resources and leadership development.
Many donors first encounter Luther through a single resource, such as an Enter the Bible article or a Faith+Lead course, and may not immediately recognize that these offerings are part of a larger network. This raises an important motivational question: Does emphasizing the breadth of Luther’s ministries increase donor confidence and willingness to give?
To explore this, we ran an A/B test on Facebook-driven traffic to two content-based donation pages:
Enter the Bible: Matthew
Faith+Lead: Genesis to Revelation Course
Visitors were randomly split between:
Control: A standard donation page featuring only the originating brand.
Treatment: The same donation page, with the addition of logos representing Luther Seminary’s affiliated ministry brands displayed prominently below the “Give” button.
The donation form defaulted to one-time giving in both versions to isolate the impact of brand presentation.
This experiment aims to answer a central donor motivation question: Does highlighting organizational scale and a family of trusted brands increase giving by reinforcing credibility and perceived impact?
The primary success metric is transactions (both one-time and monthly gifts), with revenue per visitor, one-time, and recurring gifts as secondary measures.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The overall transaction result achieved 89.3% statistical confidence, which does not meet the conventional 95% threshold for declaring a statistically significant winner. Because of this, we cannot definitively claim that the treatment caused the decrease in conversion rate. However, the results are directionally consistent across nearly every metric:
Overall conversion rate declined (-15.14%)
 	One-time giving declined (-15.51%)
 	Recurring giving declined (-34.53%, low volume)
 	Revenue per visitor declined (-7.18%)
When multiple related metrics move in the same direction, it strengthens the directional signal — even if the test does not reach full statistical significance. Directionally, this suggests that displaying multiple affiliated brand logos below the donation button likely introduced distraction or diluted the primary brand connection at the moment of giving. For Facebook-driven, content-based traffic, donors may be primarily motivated by the specific resource or brand they engaged with, rather than by the broader institutional ecosystem. At the point of conversion, clarity and singular focus may be more powerful than signals of scale.

experiments.title: How shortening the length of landing page copy impacted email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a shorter landing page, with less content and a more prominent form placement, will lead to an increase in email sign-ups by reducing friction and making it easier for visitors to find and complete the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/30/2024
Luther Seminary created an ebook on the book of Romans to engage their audience and drive sign-ups. The team wanted to test whether a shorter content offer landing page would result in more sign-ups by reducing friction for visitors, particularly by eliminating the need for them to scroll down to find the sign-up form.
The Control landing page featured a longer length, with detailed content above the fold and additional sections below the form. The Treatment landing page was shortened, focusing more succinctly on the value proposition and the sign-up form without as much content above and below.
Our hypothesis was that reducing the length of the page would reduce friction and increase sign-up conversions.
We ran this as an A/B test, with 50% of traffic seeing the longer Control page, and the other 50% seeing the shorter Treatment page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test demonstrated that a shorter landing page led to a 37.1% increase in email sign-ups, though the statistical confidence of 92.2% means the result is promising but not fully validated. Several factors may explain this outcome:
Reduced Cognitive Load & Friction: By simplifying the landing page and minimizing content above and below the form, we reduced the need for visitors to process excessive information, leading them to more easily take the desired action.
 	Clearer Path to Action: With less content to distract from the call to action, the sign-up form became the clear focus of the page, potentially prompting more visitors to complete the form.
 	User Behavior: Shorter pages can align with the behavior of web visitors who may be less patient or more likely to convert when they feel they are not overwhelmed with excessive information.
further testing with other variations of page length and content may lead to even higher conversion rates

experiments.title: Will using shorter copy on an content offer landing page increase email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using shorter, more focused copy on an acquisition landing page for website visitors interested in content offers will achieve greater rates of email acquisition because there is less friction on the page to distract from completing a form..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/30/2024
Luther Seminary has a number of ebook content offers that typically perform well for their audience. We wanted to see if we could increase the email acquisition rate on these landing pages by using more focused, concise copy compared to the standard longer control copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
The use of shorter, more focused copy on an acquisition landing page for website visitors interested in content offers resulted in a 37.1% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. The statistical level of confidence was 92.2%, which indicates a high likelihood that the increase in email acquisition was due to the change in copy length. While the results are encouraging, further testing would be necessary to ensure the validity and reliability of the findings.
 	Further analysis of the data shows a concerning drop in the average gift and overall revenue for this offer. While these metrics did not reach a statistical level of confidence, further testing should be mindful of the potential trade-off of acquiring more subscribers who are potentially less valuable to the organization.

experiments.title: Changing the default payment option for recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the default option to ACH/direct debit for donors considering a recurring gift will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the percieved greater ease for managing their ongoing gifts..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2024 - 08/23/2024
Like most nonprofit organizations, Luther Seminary places a high value on acquiring new recurring donors. Their current donation form for recurring donations defaults to asking for a credit card payment. We wanted to measure the impact of asking for a direct debit or ACH gift. The donation page copy, format, form fields, and gift array all remained identical between the control and treatment. The only variable in this experiment was the default payment option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the default payment option to ACH/direct debit for donors considering a recurring gift resulted in a significant increase in donor conversions. The treatment group saw a 458.8% increase in Recurring Donations for All Traffic compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was only 92.6%, slightly below the 95% threshold for standard significance. This means that while the results are promising and indicate that the treatment was successful, further testing is needed to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy.
While this was not the primary metric we measured, the treatment version also had a slight decrease in one-time donation conversions through this form. Since the donation page copy was focused on recurring donations, and the result was well below the level of confidence for validation, this result should not discourage future testing and optimizations for default ACH payments for recurring giving pages.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the default payment options for recurring donations to see if the initial results hold true - would offering additional methods of payment increase the conversion rate? Additionally, considering other variables such as the messaging around the payment options, the design of the donation form, and value proposition language throughout the page could affect the conversion rate. It is essential to continue experimenting and iterating to ensure that the organization is maximizing donor conversions and ultimately increasing support for Luther Seminary.

experiments.title: How the structure and design of the email acquisition page impacts email signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more designed landing page that presents the reader with a different experience showing components of the course will lead to a higher email sign-up rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2023 - 07/02/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a study guide about the Book of Psalms through paid Facebook ads. We want to increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page therefore we decided to test the design of the landing page.
Historically, we’ve tested into a more simple donation page, so with this treatment, we wanted to see if a more designed approach that presents the characteristics of the study guide in a new way would lead to more people signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which involved a more designed landing page, resulted in a 9.2% decrease in email acquisitions for all traffic compared to the control page with a 90.4% level of confidence.

2. Desktop visitors had a significant decrease of 216% in email acquisitions compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 99%.

Based on these findings, it can be concluded that the more designed landing page did not lead to a higher email sign-up rate. In fact, it had a negative impact, particularly among desktop visitors.

experiments.title: How adding specificity to your micro-value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing specific Psalms Study Guide information for study guide enrollees will achieve a higher conversion rate because readers have a clearer idea of what they will be giving to and receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
For this theological seminary client, we tested if adding more detailed copy to fund the next biblical resource can increase donor conversion rate on the current Revelation Study Guide donation page.
The control donation page copy shares that the reader’s donation will help fund the following biblical resource. We wanted to run an experiment that goes deeper into this copy and provides the reader with more information on what the following biblical resource is, ie. the Psalms Study Guide, and why it will help them, ie. “deepen their understanding of the Book of Psalms”. We have seen success with previous experiments that go deeper into their copy about what they are offering, which is why we believe this treatment will lead to a greater donor conversion rate.
This treatment experiment has been shown to 50% of the viewers and was run as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding more detailed copy on the funding of the next resource, the Psalms Study Guide, to the donation page copy did not produce a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment page saw a 66.2% decrease in conversion rate with a 90.4% confidence level, so the experiment did not validate. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may help to include an ebook mockup image on the donation page so that viewers can physically see what their donation is going towards and be more motivated to donate.

experiments.title: How increasing the ask amount on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by increasing the “ask amount” for online course enrollees will achieve an increase in average gift and revenue for this page because there will be a larger average gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
This theological seminary client offered a new biblical resource, the Revelation Online Course. The control donation page for the Revelation Online Course had a $10 donation ask in the copy. The treatment page had the original control donation page copy but also included a larger gift amount ask of a $50 or more donation. This treatment page experiment was set up to show to 50% of viewers as an A/B test. We wanted to test if asking for a larger gift amount in the treatment donation page could increase the average gift and revenue for this online course.
We have noticed in past experiments that including specific donation ask copy can produce a lift in conversion rate. We thought that this donation page copy for this new biblical resource would be a great place to ask specifically for a larger donation of $50 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specific ask amount of $50 or more to the donation page copy for the Revelation Online Course, did not lead to an increase in average gift and revenue. In fact, the treatment page saw a 56.1% decrease in conversion rate. While the statistical sample size is approaching validity, the level of confidence was 88% which did not reach above 95% to validate this experiment. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to explore alternative ways of encouraging larger donations, such as providing more details on the course they will get if they donate $50 or more.

experiments.title: How adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher users will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 10/31/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign from October 1st – October 31st. We previously tested a countdown clock on the donation page for a majority of the campaign time, but we saw a valid decrease in donor conversion with the countdown clock.
We decided to test a goal thermometer for their $50,000 campaign goal on the last 4 days of the campaign. The thermometer started at $30,000 which is how much they had raised so far. The thermometer was set to grow every time someone made a donation on that page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The goal thermometer saw a 19.5% decrease in donor conversion rate with an 89.2% level of confidence. Similar to the countdown clock, we can see from this experiment that the Working Preacher audience did not respond better to seeing the goal thermometer on the donation page.

We would like to test this concept further on another one of Luther Seminary's campaigns that's targeting a different audience so that we can try to understand if this type of "donor goal" experiment only performs negatively for the Working Preacher audience or for the Luther Seminary audience as a whole.

experiments.title: How increasing narrative affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing narrative score for previous donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/02/2022
Luther Seminary was running their biannual Working Preacher campaign. They had read some research that increasing the “narrative score” using a tool called CopyOptimizer would increase conversion rate, and wanted to put it to the test. They began with a standard appeal written by their copywriter and looked for ways to “add narrative” to it.
They decided the best way would be to put the reader in the shoes of a pastor who lacked the resources to prepare their weekly sermon. Then, they connected the appeal to provide this imaginary preacher with the resources he or she would need to teach their congregation.
When they read the treatment copy, they realized that it no longer made sense to have the thermometer image at the top. They removed that, knowing that it would add a variable to the treatment, but hoping that it would help the narrative copy make more impact.
They split their email file and send one email to each half to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 47.7% increase in conversion rate due to one, or a combination of adjustments. First, this shows that the image of the thermometer may keep people from engaging. Second—the biggest change—was to change the perspective of the reader. Instead of a

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar impacted donor conversions on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a digital acquisition donation page for prospective donors will achieve a change in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 08/01/2022
Luther Seminary was offering a free online course called Cosmic Adventure that explained the relationship between science and faith including the history behind this relationship. They offered this free course through paid Facebook ads and to their housefile through email marketing. This offer was running for almost a year and the conversion rate on the donation page was on a steep decline.
For this experiment, we wanted to see whether adding a humanized sticky bar, featuring a message and image of Andy Root, Professor at Luther Seminary and Chair of Youth and Family Ministry, would have an impact on donor conversion rates from prospective donors coming from this digital acquisition offer.
The message tried to focus the prospective donor’s attention on why they should make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a humanized sticky bar actually led to a decrease in conversion rates of 61%, but with only an 86.7% level of confidence. We hypothesize that this is due to the nature of who we included in the messaging, as well as the messaging itself. While Andy Root is well known in his community at Luther Seminary, he might not be that well known to those prospective donors who come onto the file through digital acquisition efforts--they are downloading the content because it appeals to them, and not necessarily because of who it's coming from.
Further testing on messaging and subjects will be done in the future to see if we can get a lift.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 59.9% decrease with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding tabs to an acquisition donation form affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding tabs to a donation form for the users who downloaded the offer will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2021 - 01/30/2022
For Luther Seminary’s acquisition offer, Reconnect: The Way Forward, we wanted to test the tabbed donation form on their instant donation page in order to increase the donor conversion rate. For this experiment, we kept all steps of the funnel identical in each test with the exception of changing the donation form to have a one-time donation tab and a monthly donation tab. In doing this, we removed the friction in the standard donation form that included asking the donor to check a box to make the gift recurring and asking if this donation was a change to the monthly gift they already provide.
This ebook was offered through paid Facebook advertising and the housefile email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this experiment did not reach the 95% level of confidence standard to be valid, we saw a directional lift in conversion rate on the tabbed donation form. We believe this happened because we removed the friction within the form causing the overall donation experience to be shorter and more organized for the donor.

This concept can be tested on any acquisition donation page or main donation page, and we believe this will increase the donor conversion rate.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 58.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 220.2% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How radically simplifying the email format affects engagement and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile will achieve greater immediate and downstream engagement because readers will perceive greater authenticity and trust, since the email appears to have been sent from an individual rather than a list.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2026 - 02/11/2026
Luther Seminary has been sending emails without many design elements to their housefile for years, believing that the more a message looks as if it was sent by an individual, the greater the trust and engagement will be. While their standard email template does not appear to have many design elements, there is still a bit of extra white space and unconventional line spacing that could subconsciously lead a reader to associate the message with marketing.
We wanted to see if fully simplifying email formatting would boost engagement. For the test, we removed all padding. We matched font, size, and color to a typical personal email.
We determined that click-through rate would gauge impact. We also measured offer sign-ups and donations to assess how a front-end change affected downstream behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Top of Funnel: Email Clicks Radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile did not lead to greater immediate engagement. The treatment actually led to a slight -2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control, though the difference was not statistically significant and therefore unreliable.

Mid Funnel: Offer Sign-Ups. The control email yielded 317 offer sign-ups, and the treatment yielded 316. The radically simplified email format had no impact on downstream offer sign-ups for email subscribers.

Bottom of Funnel: Donations. The treatment email resulted in a 40% increase in donations, a 48% increase in overall revenue, and a 5% increase in average gift, compared to the control, at a 85.8% level of confidence.

Key Takeaway: While the experiment did not yield the top-of-funnel increase in click-through rate as predicted, the impact on downstream donations warrants further testing. The greater authenticity of the radically simplified design could affect donor perceptions of the sender and carry through to important downstream actions. More experiments could demonstrate a clearer link and answer additional questions, such as: How does a radically simplified email format impact engagement in other email types, such as a direct appeal or cultivation message?

experiments.title: Does Making the “Last Name” Field Optional Increase Email Sign-Ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the “Last Name” field optional on content offer sign-up forms will increase email acquisition because reducing required effort lowers friction at the moment of decision and makes the form feel simpler and less intrusive to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/24/2026
Luther Seminary uses its Illustrated Book of Acts and Enter the Bible Exodus content offers as ongoing acquisition tools to introduce new audiences to its ministry and grow its email list. These resources consistently attract individuals interested in biblical scholarship and theological education and serve as an important entry point into long term engagement.
As the team reviewed the landing page experience for these offers, they identified a potential source of friction within the sign up form. The form required three fields to be completed: first name, last name, and email address. While collecting a full name supports personalization and database management, required fields can introduce subtle resistance, especially for individuals who are early in their relationship with the organization.
This raised an important motivational question. When someone is downloading free theological content, are they willing to provide only the minimum required information, and does requiring a last name create enough additional effort or privacy concern to reduce completion rates?
Rather than removing the last name field entirely, Luther Seminary tested making the field optional while still requiring first name and email address. This approach preserved the opportunity to collect full names from those willing to provide them while lowering the minimum amount of required information needed to complete the form.
Two split URL tests were conducted, one for Illustrated Book of Acts and one for Enter the Bible Exodus. In each case, the page experience was identical except for the required fields on the form.
Control
First name required
Last name required
Email required
Treatment
First name required
Last name optional
Email required
Because both offers tested the same structural change with similar acquisition audiences, the results were aggregated to evaluate the overall impact of reducing required form fields across content campaigns.
The primary metric was email acquisition measured by form completion rate. Secondary metrics included overall transactions, one time gifts, recurring gifts, and revenue to ensure that any improvement in top of funnel performance did not negatively affect downstream donor value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test reached 77.2% statistical confidence, which does not meet the 95% level of confidence threshold for a valid learning. Therefore, we cannot conclude that making the “Last Name” field optional had a meaningful impact on email acquisition. The observed differences between control and treatment may be due to normal variation rather than a true behavioral effect.

experiments.title: How using No-primed questions in a survey impacted engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using “No-primed” questions for a survey offer will achieve an increase in survey completions (email submissions) because respondents will feel a greater sense of autonomy and control over their answers, which may lead to a subconscious desire to resolve with a “Yes” when asked to contribute later.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2024 - 11/27/2024
During high-urgency campaigns such as calendar year-end, we often use a survey as a tool to help “prime” Luther Seminary’s audience with knowledge about the problems that Luther Seminary is working to solve through their fundraising campaign. Typically, these survey questions are “yes-primed”, which means that they’re designed so that a Luther Seminary supporter would respond “Yes” to each question.
For Luther Seminary’s calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test this “yes-primed” framing against a survey that was “no-primed”. Our goal was to understand how this “Yes” or “No” priming impacts engagement and downstream donor behavior.
Both survey funnels were identical, with the only change being the phrasing of the 5 survey questions. The Control survey used “Yes”-primed questions, which are designed to elicit affirmative responses from respondents. The Treatment survey, on the other hand, used “No”-primed questions, encouraging respondents to think critically about the topics and more likely to answer negatively.
This experiment aimed to explore the psychological principle of reactance theory, which suggests that when people feel their freedom of choice is being restricted or guided (such as through overly positive framing), they may react by resisting or disengaging. Conversely, by using “No”-primed questions, we hypothesized that respondents would feel a greater sense of autonomy and control over their answers. The idea is that when people are encouraged to think critically and reject easy affirmations, they may feel more invested in their responses, fostering a stronger connection with the survey.
Additionally, answering “No” to these questions may create a cognitive dissonance effect, where respondents feel the need to resolve the negative stance with a positive action, such as donating after completing the survey. This subconscious desire for resolution may lead to higher engagement during the survey and increased donor conversions later in the funnel, especially when presented with an opportunity to act on their beliefs.
We split traffic to the survey pages evenly, and measured the # of survey submissions (emails acquired), as well as downstream donations on the post-survey donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test can be broken down into 3 key learnings:

 

#1 - Increase in Engagement with “No”-Primed Questions

The Treatment survey, which used "No"-primed questions, resulted in a 7.8% increase in form submissions compared to the Control survey. While this increase did not meet the 95% statistical level of confidence (79.1% LOC), it still suggests a promising trend toward higher engagement. This aligns with our hypothesis, which was grounded in reactance theory. By encouraging respondents to critically evaluate the questions and answer negatively, we likely fostered a greater sense of autonomy. Respondents who felt they were not being led to affirmations may have felt more invested in their responses, leading to increased engagement.

 

#2 - Potential Impact of “No” Priming on Conversion Behavior

Despite the increase in form submissions, the Conversion Rate for the Treatment group (2.41%) was slightly lower than the Control group (3.36%). This discrepancy suggests that while “No”-primed questions led to more people completing the survey, there might be a short-term resistance to following through with donations immediately after completing the survey. However, the cognitive dissonance effect mentioned in our hypothesis may still be at play—respondents who answered "No" to the questions may feel a subconscious need to resolve the negativity, and this could influence them to donate in the future as they reconcile their answers with the desire to support Luther Seminary.

 

#3 - Higher Average Gift in the Treatment Group

The Treatment group had a higher average gift of $32, compared to the Control group’s $23. This finding suggests that while the number of donors was slightly lower in the Treatment group, those who did choose to give were more likely to contribute larger amounts. This is potentially indicative of commitment consistency. Respondents who were engaged through the "No"-primed survey, and who felt more autonomous in their responses, may have been more motivated to make a larger, more deliberate contribution to Luther Seminary's cause. By thinking critically about their answers, they may have been more intentional in their giving.

 

The results of this experiment point toward the potential effectiveness of “No”-primed surveys for increasing engagement and stimulating larger gifts, even if immediate conversions didn’t significantly increase. Respondents who answered negatively may have felt more empowered and invested in their decisions, which could lead to higher-value gifts in the long term. Future tests could explore the extended impact of these psychological principles, with a focus on donor behavior over time and refining the optimal balance between engagement and conversion.

experiments.title: How adding emphasis on recurring giving impacts recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing recurring giving for donation page vistors will achieve more recurring gifts because it encourages donors to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2024 - 07/18/2024
Luther Seminary was running their Fiscal Year End campaign. While writing copy for this campaign, we decided we wanted to test how emphasizing recurring giving on their FYE donation page giving impacted recurring giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing recurring giving on the donation page led to an increase in recurring donations for Luther Seminary. Despite the positive outcome, the results are not statistically significant with only a 43.3% level of confidence. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. This will help in making informed decisions based on reliable data.

experiments.title: How an actionable and enticing subject line impacts clicks

rows.name: Connection with God, Neighbors, and Community
three steps to deepen your relationships…

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more actionable subject line in a cultivation housefile email will achieve more clicks because it create more curiosity .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2024 - 02/21/2024
Luther Seminary sent an article in a cultivation email about three steps to deepen your connection with God, neighbors, and community. We tested using a more actionable subject like impacted clicks to see if it encouraged more people to click to read the article.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which used a more actionable subject line, did not perform better than the control in terms of Clicks for All Traffic. In fact, it experienced a 2.5% decrease in clicks.

3. The statistical level of confidence for the Clicks for All Traffic results was only 22.9%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experiments, it is important to consider the following:

1. While creating more actionable subject lines may increase open rates, it may not necessarily lead to more clicks. It is important to strike a balance between creating curiosity and providing clear information in the subject line.

2. It is crucial to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence in the results before making any conclusions or decisions based on the outcome of the experiment.

3. Further experimentation and testing may be needed to fully understand the impact of different subject line strategies on email performance.

experiments.title: How adding a “Most Popular Gift” call out on the gift array impacts one-time donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a “Most Popular Gift” call out for donation page gift arrays will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is.. because  it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2024 - 02/23/2024
Luther Seminary has seen a steady amount of traffic on their donation page. Conversions have been consistent, but the average gift has been lower. We wanted to see if we could get more conversions and also increase the number of gifts because we increase clarity of what the most popular option is for most page visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "Most Popular Gift" call out to the donation page gift arrays led to a 22.0% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical confidence level of 77.3% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and unreliable for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and account for any potential fluctuations.
Overall, the concept of highlighting a "Most Popular Gift" option shows promise in potentially increasing donor conversions and gift amounts. It could be worth conducting further experiments with adjustments to ensure reliable and conclusive results that can guide decision-making for optimizing donation pages in the future.

experiments.title: How communal language on a pop up impacts donor conversion during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using communal language for the campaign pop up will achieve an increase in donations because it increases the appeal of making a gift by emphasizing the reader’s role in the work being done.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2023 - 01/01/2024
Luther Seminary ran a campaign during the month of December. For this campaign, we wanted to see how the concept of communal language impacted overall donor conversion.
For this test, we created a control pop-up that clearly outlines the problem and the solution that Luther Seminary provides. For the treatment, we emphasized that the reader was an important member of the Luther Community, reminding them that their support is essential to the work that Luther is doing. Then it explains what the donor’s gift will do.
Both pop-ups led to the same donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using communal language in the campaign pop-up resulted in a slight increase in donations compared to the control pop-up. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it would be important to run the test for a longer duration to collect more data and increase the statistical significance. This will ensure that the results are reliable and can be used for making informed decisions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the communal language to find the most effective messaging that resonates with donors.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar on acquisition donation pages impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a sticky bar for newly acquired names will achieve a higher conversion rate because the donation ask is enforced with year end motivations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2023 - 12/28/2023
Belmont Abbey College is a Catholic university located in North Carolina.
During calendar year-end, we decided to test the presence of a sticky bar on acquisition offer donation pages encouraging potential donors to make a year-end gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a persuasive message on acquisition offer donation pages can potentially increase the conversion rate. The treatment version, which included the sticky bar with a specific call-to-action and a motivational message, resulted in a 31.5% increase in transactions for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be important to gather a larger sample size or run the experiment for a longer duration to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. A higher level of confidence is essential to ensure that any changes made based on the experiment results are valid and reliable.
Additionally, it would be worth considering testing variations of the sticky bar message, design, or placement to find the most effective combination. Exploring different strategies and optimizing the implementation of sticky bars can potentially lead to improved conversion rates and better engagement with potential donors.
Overall, this experiment provides a valuable insight into the potential impact of adding a sticky bar on acquisition offer donation pages. However, further experimentation is needed to validate and refine the results before implementing any changes on a larger scale.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a campaign donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page for users who are familiar with this organization will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because Jake is a familiar face to these subscribers which will increase the humanization of the donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/01/2023
This ministry client was running a campaign for their fiscal year-end. We’ve seen humanized sticky bars increase donor conversion for other organizations when used for high-urgency campaigns, so we decided to test this on their main campaign donation page which received traffic from their housefile list and Facebook ads.
The sticky bar included a message from Jake Hennes, someone who the file is very familiar with and who they receive cultivation emails from often. We wanted the urgent, important message to come from someone they recognize. Jake is also the one who sent a majority of the campaign emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a humanized sticky bar featuring a familiar face can significantly impact donor conversion rates, especially for users who are already familiar with the organization.

2. The treatment resulted in a 25.6% increase in donations for all traffic, suggesting that the humanization of the donation ask through the use of a familiar face resonates positively with donors.

3. Female visitors, in particular, showed a significant increase in donations, with a whopping 956% increase and a 95% level of confidence. This suggests that the humanized sticky bar was especially effective in engaging and persuading female donors.

4. While the results of the experiment showed promising improvements in donor conversion rates, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended threshold of 85%, but due to the volume of gifts and difference in donor conversion, we believe this concept should be used in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a ebook acquisition page affects sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof for online course enrollees will achieve a higher email sign up rate because the social proof shows the user that there are others who have downloaded the ebook and it offers them a chance to join a group of others with similar interests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2023 - 07/02/2023
This ministry client is offering a free online course on the book of Psalms through paid Facebook ads. We wanted to increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page, so we decided to test adding the concept of social proof. We added social proof by adding a floating white box that says “We’ve already had over 3,000 people sign up for this online course! Will you join the group of people who are expanding their knowledge of the Book of Psalms?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding social proof for the online course resulted in a 10.1% increase in email acquisitions with a 75.1% level of confidence. This indicates that social proof has a positive impact on encouraging users to sign up.

2. When analyzing the results based on gender, it was found that female visitors had a significant 681% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that the addition of social proof had a strong and positive impact specifically on female visitors.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to validate the impact of social proof on overall email sign-up rates.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options in the gift array affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of options on the gift array for ebook downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there are fewer decisions to make at the point of conversion, overall reducing the amount of friction involved in the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2023 - 06/09/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a study guide about the Book of Revelation. Once users download the free study guide, they are redirected to the instant donation page where they are presented with value proposition asking for a donation to Luther Seminary.
Luther Seminary wanted to increase the instant conversion rate on this page so we decided to test only having one option on the gift array versus normally having four options. We believe this will decrease the friction involved in the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of options on the gift array for ebook downloaders did not achieve a higher donor conversion rate in this particular case. The treatment group saw a decrease of 33.4% in conversion rate with a level of confidence of 61%, making the results invalid. Because this experiment did not have 50 donations minimum and did not reach a valid level of confidence, it's hard to say how this would affect other offers and organizations.

In future experimentation, we need to test this hypothesis on an offer that receives more traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How using language around gaining deeper knowledge and enhancing their faith affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language centered around gaining a deeper knowledge of the book of Psalms and enhancing their faith for potential online course signees will achieve a higher email conversion rate because it taps into the inherent desire for personal growth, intellectual curiosity, and a sense of accomplishment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2023 - 06/15/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a free online course of the Book of Psalms. They wanted to try and increase the email conversion rate on the sign-up page for the course, so we decided to test using language around gaining a deeper knowledge of this book of the Bible while enhancing their faith. We did this in two ways:
Emphasis on Knowledge and Understanding: The treatment page focuses on the idea of gaining deeper knowledge and understanding of the Book of Psalms. By highlighting the informative and insightful nature of the course, it appeals to individuals who are seeking to expand their knowledge base and develop a more profound understanding of the Psalms. This emphasis on learning and personal growth can be compelling for those who value educational opportunities.
Cultivating Personal Qualities: The first page mentions that studying the Psalms can help cultivate qualities such as a heart of worship, hunger for God, authentic prayer life, and a thankful heart. This emphasis on personal transformation and spiritual development appeals to individuals who are looking for practical applications and benefits from studying the Psalms. It positions the course as a means to enhance one’s relationship with God and deepen their faith.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to test the effectiveness of using language centered around gaining a deeper knowledge of the book of Psalms and enhancing faith resulted in a 6.5% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic with a 73.3% level of confidence. However, this result cannot be considered valid for future decision making as the level of confidence is below 85%.

The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing knowledge and understanding or cultivating personal qualities may not be effective for all visitors. Therefore, it is important to understand the audience and tailor language accordingly. Future experiments should continue to test various messaging approaches, target specific audiences, and gather feedback to determine what messaging is most effective in increasing email acquisition.

experiments.title: How adding specificity to your micro-value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing specific Psalms Study Guide information for course enrollees will achieve a higher conversion rate because readers have a clearer idea of what they will be giving to and receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 05/07/2023
This theological seminary client offers a few online courses as a biblical resource. On the Psalms Course resource, we wanted to test specific language in the copy of the donation page to see if it would produce a higher conversion rate.
The control donation page copy includes a donation ask that shares that the reader’s donation will help fund the next published biblical resource. We wanted to run an experiment that goes deeper into this copy and provides the reader with more information on what the following biblical resource is, ie. the Psalms Study Guide, and why it will help them, ie. “deepen their understanding of the Book of Psalms”.
This treatment experiment has been shown to 50% of the viewers and was run as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding more detailed language to fund their next biblical resource, the Psalms Study Guide, to the donation page copy did not produce a higher donation conversion rate. In fact, the treatment page saw a 6% decrease in conversion rate but with a 29.9% confidence level, so the experiment did not validate. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may help to include specific details about what the donor will read, ie. chapter topics, so the donor will have a better expectation of what they will be receiving if they donate.

experiments.title: How adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion

rows.name: Campaign Focused Donation Page - With Thermometer

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the goal thermometer on a donation page leverages psychological factors such as visual progress, social proof, competitiveness, accountability, and psychological commitment and engages potential donors on an emotional level and provides them with a compelling reason to contribute to the cause.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/26/2023
Luther Seminary is running its bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. They wanted to test whether adding a goal thermometer to the donation page would increase donor conversion.
We added the thermometer to the treatment page from the start of the campaign and showed it to 50% of traffic. Users came to this donation page through email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that adding a goal thermometer to a donation page for Working Preacher subscribers resulted in a 4.6% increase in donations with a 42.1% level of confidence. While the experiment suggests that adding a goal thermometer may be effective in increasing donations, further testing is needed to confirm this.

We believe this did not validate as an increase in donor conversion because the Working Preacher subscribers are already comfortable and emotionally driven to support Working Preacher. Therefore they did not need the extra push that the thermometer provided.

experiments.title: How asking existing donors for a recurring donation on an instant donation page affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the value proposition to a recurring donation ask on an instant donation page for existing donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because these people are donors and have shown that they care about this organization in the past.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2023 - 04/01/2023
Luther Seminary is wanting to increase the number of recurring donors they have. In order to try and get existing donors to convert to recurring donors, we decided to target their existing donors with Facebook ads that advertised their free content offers. They typically offer study guides or online courses.
We decided that 50% of traffic would see the regular instant donation page that includes value proposition around funding the next resource, and the other 50% of traffic would see an instant donation page with value proposition centered around becoming a monthly donor with the recurring tab preselected.
We made sure to only target donors by setting the audience targeting to include traffic when the utm_content contains “donors” (the name of the asset).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The recurring ask instant donation page saw a 3.8% lift in recurring conversion with a 69.2% level of confidence. But this is only factoring in one gift.

We did see a 74.4% decrease in overall giving with a 97.6% level of confidence for one-time donations.

Due to this experiment running for over a month, we decided to turn it off. The traffic to this experiment was very low because this ad was in a multi-offer campaign specifically targeting existing donors.

Although we did not see a valid increase or decrease in recurring giving, we do believe the recurring ask negatively affected giving all together. We would like to test this experiment again for an offer that is receiving more traffic.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition to focus on “more content like this” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the value proposition on “more content like this” for study guide downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because specificity helps decrease cognitive friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2023 - 02/27/2023
Luther Seminary was getting great responses for their free study guide on the book of Revelation in terms of email signups. However, they wanted to see if they could increase their instant donor conversion rate.
In reviewing the value proposition on their donation page, they noticed that the reasons to give that were given were very high-level, wide-ranging, and broad. They hypothesized that if they focused the value proposition on just one element—particularly, producing more biblical resources like what the user had just signed up for, they could improve donor conversion rate.
So, they drafted a new donation page with this “micro-value-proposition” and launched a test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for a month, the treatment page saw a 93.2% increase in donor conversion rate with a 76.9% level of confidence.

This experiment demonstrated that Luther Seminary might significantly increase its donation rate by focusing the value proposition on a single element — in this case, the biblical resources it promotes.

*However, it must be noted that this experiment was limited in its promotion due to budget, and thus only 10 total donations were included. While the gap in conversion rates was significant, it did not reach NextAfter's desired threshold of 50 donations.

experiments.title: How removing thank you from the headline on the instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing thank you from the headline on the instant donation page for users who downloaded the free study guide will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2022 - 12/19/2022
Luther Seminary is offering a free study guide to learn about the Book of Proverbs. We had previously seen other organizations test the concept of removing “thank you” from the header of the donation page as that typically ends a user’s thought process when signing up for something or completing a purchase. Usually, when you see the thank you confirmation page, you think to yourself – Okay, I’m done now, so I’m leaving the page.
We predicted that users are leaving the donation page after seeing “thank you” at the very top, so we want to test changing that language to something that lets the user know that they are not done yet.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page that didn't state thank you in the headline saw a 17.8% lift in conversion rate with only a 38.4% level of confidence. Revenue saw a  10.6% lift with only a 60.8% level of confidence.
This experiment was running for over two months therefore we concluded that it was not going to validate either way. We believe this experiment tells us that removing thank you from the headline did not end the user experience like we initially thought it did.
We also ran this same experiment on another study guide for Revelation and saw similar results. Because this did not produce a decrease in donations or revenue, we rolled the treatment page out to 100% of traffic.

experiments.title: How beginning an email with a declaration vs a personalized sentence affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a more personalized sentence rather than a declaration for email openers will achieve a higher click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
Luther Seminary was running their year-end high-urgency campaign, and for the first two appeal emails of the campaign, they wanted to test if starting the email with a more personal sentence instead of a declaration would keep users reading longer which would ultimately increase the click-through rate.
The control email started off with an explicit statement that could be viewed as not personal or more “organizational”. The treatment email started with a personal statement that sounds more conversational or like you’re speaking with someone, not just an organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment email saw a 10.1% decrease in click-through rate with only a 57.7% level of confidence. Because of this, we can assume that starting an email with a declaration or personalized statement does not affect the click-through rate of the email. We believe this does not have as much of an impact because it's changing something very small in the email when compared to the overall value proposition the rest of the email provides.

experiments.title: How acknowledging someones previous gift history affects giving in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out previous giving history for subscribers and donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 11/29/2022
For Luther Seminary’s Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to test how calling out the user’s previous giving history would affect the conversion rate. To do this, we split their file into four segments: never donors, lapsed donors, lybunt donors, and active donors. After those were segmented, we split traffic 50/50 where the control segment saw language around “someone who supports the mission of Luther Seminary” and the treatment segment saw language that specifically called out their last gift DATE and last gift AMOUNT. (Or for never donors, specifically calling out that they had never given a gift to Luther Seminary before.)
We ran this experiment on the AM and PM emails with the same segmentation for Giving Tuesday. In order to have the potential to reach statistical validation, all results have been complied into this one experiment write up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email saw a -.02% decrease in conversion rate with only a .05% level of confidence. These results tell us that there was no difference in giving because of calling out previous donor history.
We would like to test this concept on a different high-urgency appeal email during a time of year when the motivation to give is not as high.

experiments.title: How using a clear call-to-action focused on taking the next step of giving a donation in a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using clear call-to-action language  for subscribers and donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2022 - 12/31/2022
As a part of Luther Seminary’s calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test the concept of using a clear call-to-action to give a donation vs using a more vague call-to-action of “learn more” to see how it affects donor conversion from the email to the donation page.
We believe that using a clear call-to-action that focuses on taking the next step is most effective as focusing on something vague like “learn more” doesn’t set the right expectation on the next page (donation page).
For this experiment, we tested this concept throughout the final four appeal emails of this campaign in order to receive enough traffic to have the possibility of reaching a valid level of confidence.
The control emails used clear call-to-action language to make a donation and the treatment emails used vague call-to-action language around “learn more”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment emails using the vague call-to-action saw a 14% increase in conversion rate with only a 35.3% level of confidence therefore the clear call-to-action did not have an effect on the users decision to give.
We believe this happened because the motivation for year-end giving is already higher than all other times of the year. Because of this, using vague or clear language for the call-to-action did not effect whether someone was going to make a donation or not. It's also safe to say that there was not confusion between the vague call-to-action in the treatment email and the donation page.
We would like to test this concept again on another appeal email outside of calendar year-end.

experiments.title: How distinguishing between two topics in an email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that distinguishing between an offer and an incentive for Working Preacher donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how distinguishing between the two topics in an email (the match for the campaign first, then the free content offer, Sermon Brainwave next) would affect the conversion rate.
The original email seemed jumbled between the incentive (Serman Brainwave content unlock) and the offer (matching gift).
experiments.keyLearnings:
As you can see in the chart above, distinguishing between the offer and incentive did not have an impact on the conversion rate for this email. The treatment saw a 3.8% lift with only an 11.5% level of confidence.

We believe the Working Preacher audience either did not need the extra clarification between the offer and incentive in the email or we did not distinguish enough between the two topics which caused no change in the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How sticking to urgency in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sticking to urgency and removing extended value proposition for the final email in a high urgency campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how sticking with urgency and reducing the amount of extended value proposition copy in the final email would affect donor conversion.
We did this by removing the extra language from the control email centered around what their gift would do for Working Preacher, because these points had been conveyed heavily in every email in the campaign prior to the final email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As you can see in the chart above, removing the extended value proposition and focusing mainly on urgency did not have an impact on the conversion rate for this email. The treatment saw a 6.6% decrease with only a 20.2% level of confidence.

From this experiment, we can assume that the difference in language did not affect the donor's decision to give at this point in the campaign. We believe this is because both emails had the urgency of that being the final day, and whether or not we included the extended value proposition, it did not affect the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How deriving impact back to the donor affects donor conversion rate in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that deriving the impact back to the donor for potential donors apart of the Working Preacher campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how deriving impact back to the donor instead of the organization would impact the results of one email.
We A/B tested this concept on email 3 of the campaign by changing the email copy from language focused on what Working Preacher will do if they gain support to the specific impact the donor will have on pastors and Christians with their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we did not receive enough traffic on this email to see the validity, we saw a decrease of 24.1% in conversion rate with a 53.2% level of confidence. We believe this happened because Working Preacher donors are more inclined to give to this project when they know how the organization is providing and giving back.

By taking that concept away in the treatment and changing the narrative to what the donor will do with their gift, Working Preacher donors did not get the reassurance they needed to see what Working Preacher would do with their support.

In order to be confident in these results, we'll need to further test this concept with the Working Preacher audience.

experiments.title: How sending users to a campaign specific donation page during a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a campaign specific donation page for people in a high urgency campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2022 - 10/20/2022
Luther Seminary is running the bi-annual Working Preacher campaign.
Previously during high-urgency campaigns, they have always sent their users to the main donation page on workingpreacher.org which has many friction opportunities (navigation away from the page) and limited value proposition.
They wanted to test building a completely separate campaign donation that has more value proposition on why someone should give to the Working Preacher campaign and what Working Preacher will do for you (the donor).
We set up this experiment with the new copy and removed the extra friction (navigation) from the page as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for over 17 days with more than 630 samples to test with. All in all, we did not see any significant changes in performance between the control donation page and the campaign-specific donation page. We only saw a decrease in conversion rate of 3.8% with only a 29% level of confidence.

We believe this is because they have a very targeted and invested audience of Working Preacher members and donors therefore adding the extra value proposition or removing the extra friction did not make an overall impact on the users' decision to give to this campaign.

We will do further testing into the campaign-specific donation page by adding a countdown clock to provide more urgency toward the deadline.

experiments.title: How adding a deadline to a pop-up affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a deadline to a pop-up for users on the Working Preacher website will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2022 - 11/04/2022
Luther Seminary was running their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. We added a pop-up to the Working Preacher website and wanted to test how adding urgency / the deadline to the pop-up copy would affect donor conversion rate for users coming to the donation page from that source.
We added the deadline of October 31st to the headline and the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for about a month and saw an 8.7% lift in conversion rate with only a 60.5% level of confidence. We believe that adding the deadline to the pop-up provided more clarity for the users on the front end of what we were asking them to do and when we needed the donation.

Because this did not validate, we would like to further test this concept on pop-ups for future high-urgency campaigns.

Something else to note is that we should have tracked clicked conversion as well, to see if adding the deadline affected click-through rate. On our next test, we will need to track the clicks on the button as well.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition language on the instant donation page for prospective donors will achieve clarity and cause an increase in ICR.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2022 - 09/18/2022
Luther Seminary has been running paid Facebook ads and email marketing for their content offer Enter the Bible: Revelation Study Guide. While the email sign-up rate was healthy around 48%, we wanted to try and increase the instant conversion rate on the donation page.
We hypothesized that the copy on the control page did not provide clarity on the impact a donation has for Luther Seminary and what the donor’s gift will do for the organization.
We believed that adding more value proposition-focused language to the page would provide clarity to the prospective donor for how their gift will be used in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the experiment were encouraging when it comes to lifts in revenue for this acquisition offer. We saw a 39.1% increase in average gift with a lift of 75.4% level of confidence. This experiment had been running for over a month and had not been validated therefore we moved traffic to the treatment page with confidence that the value prop copy will increase the giving amount on this page.
While we saw a lift in average gift, the results for donor conversion rate were flat at 4.3% lift with a 7.34% level of confidence.
We believe that the value proposition copied inspired potential donors more to give a higher gift, but was not enough to convince someone to give if they already decided against it. Although it did not negatively affect the decision to make a gift.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by changing the confirmation message at the top of the page to be more clarifying following the previous step in the funnel. We will still want to verify these results by running this experiment on another instant donation page while isolating the variables being tested.

experiments.title: How adding campaign-level specificity affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding campaign-level specificity for website visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2021 - 12/04/2021
Luther Seminary was running a Fall Fundraising Drive for their Working Preacher site. A popup had been generating a significant amount of revenue for them, but they wanted to see if they could increase the conversion rate.
The control asked the visitor to help pastors in need. They wondered if being more specific about the campaign and the goal, changing the recipient from the pastor to the site would increase conversion rate, based on the fact that the visitor had affinity with the website.
They crafted an alternate popup and split the traffic to see if it produced a conversion increase.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the popup did not move the needle, generating only a 7.6% lift with a 41% level of significance. Digging into the segment-level data, this new copy did appear to convert slightly better for NEW visitors, but not significant either.

experiments.title: How personalizing the Working Preacher fall campaign email effected the donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing an email for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/17/2021
This experiment was run on the first email sent to the housefile for the Working Preacher fall campaign.
The goal of this experiment was to increase donor conversion rate by personalizing the verbiage and simplifying the design of the email.
Within the treatment, we changed multiple factors including:
Beginning the email with a personalized story of how Working Preacher has positively affected a preacher’s job and messages
Explaining the why behind how coming up with new and interesting content, week-after-week, can be very difficult and strenuous
Simplifying the design and removing the templated “look” of the email to seem like it’s coming from a real person
Changing the wording from (in the control) speaking from an organization to (in the treatment) speaking in a conversation
Putting more emphasis on the donor and how important their donation is to this campaign
Adding more hyperlinks throughout the whole email to keep giving a donation at the top of mind for the potential donor
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that personalizing an email that is asking for donations to the Working Preacher fall campaign had no effect on the donor conversion rate.

We believe that Working Preacher's donors are already accustomed to how Working Preacher talks throughout their campaigns, so personalizing the email did not have an effect on the donor's motivation to give.
